lessons.title: Don't Put All Your Eggs in One Basket
lessons.pitfall: Putting more than 20% of your money into a single company
why_risky: Big losses from one company: If one stock you own drops 20%, your whole investment drops 8% - that's too much
No backup plan: When one company has problems, you feel it deeply
Risky rides: Your money bounces around too much
Bad news hits hard: One company's bad news wipes out a big chunk of your money

real_example.scenario: A Tesla fan invests too much in one company
real_example.problem: When Tesla stock fell 30%, the whole investment dropped 12%
real_example.lesson: Having other investments helped reduce the damage. But Tesla being too big made it worse

solution.rule: Never put more than 10-15% into any single company
solution.why_it_works: If one company's stock drops 50%, your total investment only drops 5-7.5%
implementation: Rule: Don't spend more than 15% of your money on one company
Example: If you have $10,000, put no more than $1,500 in any one stock
Every 3 months: Check if one stock grew too big, and sell some to balance

lessons.title: Spread Across Different Industries
lessons.pitfall: Having too much money in one industry (40%+ in the same sector)
why_risky: One bad year for an industry = your money drops: If tech stocks crash, your whole portfolio crashes
Missing winners: If healthcare does great, you miss out because you're mostly in tech
No protection: Different industries move at different times - that's what saves you
Double trouble: Tech stocks are easy to like, so you accidentally own too many

real_example.scenario: An investor loved tech companies in 2022
real_example.problem: When tech crashed 33% that year, their money dropped 25% (market only dropped 18%)
real_example.lesson: Adding healthcare or utility stocks would have softened the blow

solution.rule: Don't put more than 30-35% into any one industry
implementation: List all your companies and group them by industry
Find industries where you have more than 30%
Buy stocks from industries you're weak in
Use funds (like VTI) to automatically spread across all industries

lessons.title: Don't Bet Everything on Your Top 3
lessons.pitfall: Your top 3 companies make up more than 50% of your total investment
why_risky: Too much riding on 3 bets: If your top 3 don't work out, everything falls apart
One bad guess ruins you: If you picked wrong for those 3 companies, you lose big
High risk, same reward: Taking more risk doesn't mean you make more money
Can't sleep at night: Your money swings wildly, causing stress

real_example.scenario: An investor believed strongly in 3 tech companies
real_example.problem: Top 3 = 70% of their money. When Apple had supply problems, the portfolio dropped 8% in one day
real_example.lesson: The 30% in other companies wasn't enough to cushion the blow

solution.rule: Your top 3 stocks should be less than 50% (better if less than 40%)
solution.why_it_works: This way, if you're wrong about one company, the others can help
implementation: Add up your 3 biggest companies
If it's more than 50%, sell some of the biggest ones
Buy more different stocks to spread the risk
Aim for 15-30 different companies total

lessons.title: Own Enough Different Companies
lessons.pitfall: You only own fewer than 10 different stocks
why_risky: One company's bad news hurts a lot: With only 5 stocks, one bad report drops your money 3-4%
No room for winners: With 5 stocks, you miss out on finding the next big winner
Too much work: It's hard to keep track of just 5 companies well
Science says: Research shows 10-15 stocks is the minimum for safety

real_example.scenario: A casual investor picks just 5 big companies
real_example.problem: When Nvidia missed its sales target, the portfolio dropped 4% in one day
real_example.lesson: With 15 stocks instead of 5, the same news would have only dropped it 1%

solution.alternative: Or use a fund (like VTI) that owns 1000s of companies automatically
implementation: Start small: Pick 10-15 good companies you understand
Add gradually: Every 3 months, add 2-3 new companies
Spread around: Make sure they're from different industries
Or use a fund: One fund can give you instant ownership of hundreds of companies

lessons.title: Don't Chase Last Year's Winners
lessons.pitfall: Buying stocks that did best last year
why_risky: Winners don't always repeat: The best stock last year often doesn't win next year
Buying at the peak: You're buying when the price is highest
Bad timing: You usually buy right before things get worse
Lose money: Studies show people who chase winners lose 2-3% per year

real_example.holdings: Bought: Nvidia, Tesla, ARK fund (all were huge winners in 2021)
real_example.result: Nvidia dropped 50%, Tesla dropped 65%, ARK dropped 66% in 2022
real_example.lesson: Last year's winners became 2022's worst performers

solution.rule: Ignore last year's best performers. Look for beaten-down stocks instead
solution.approach: Buy companies that did poorly but have good fundamentals
implementation: Ignore top performer lists
Look for good companies selling cheap
Buy low performers that have improved their business
Buy gradually over 3-6 months, don't rush

lessons.red_flag_threshold: All stocks up more than 25% last year
lessons.warning_threshold: Average stock up more than 15% last year

lessons.title: Balance Stocks and Bonds by Age
lessons.pitfall: Holding 100% stocks when you should have some bonds, or too many bonds when you're young
why_risky: All stocks = scary rides: 100% stocks means wild swings, tempting you to panic sell
Too many bonds = missing growth: Too many safe bonds means you miss out on long-term gains
No cushion: Stocks and bonds move differently - bonds protect when stocks fall
Wrong for your age: A 25-year-old shouldn't have the same mix as a 65-year-old

real_example.scenario: A 30-year-old with all their money in stocks during the 2020 crash
real_example.problem: Got scared and sold at the bottom, missing the recovery
real_example.lesson: 20% bonds would have only dropped it 27%, and wouldn't feel so scary

solution.rule: Use this simple rule: Bonds = 100 minus your age
implementation: Buy a low-cost bond fund (like BND or AGG)
Set your mix based on your age
Check once per year and rebalance
Bonds give you 'dry powder' to buy stocks when they're cheap

lessons.warning_threshold: Imbalance more than 20% from your age-based target
